RT @austinyorski: HUGE plot hole in reality: every person carries around a device with access to the totality of human knowledge and yet pe…
@AdamInOakland And I think that I will live to see the day an officer pulls another back, refuses to teargas, or whistleblows wrongdoing.
@AdamInOakland Again, I agree that in all of the police relations I've seen so far, not one officer has done anything in a range of 'good'.
@AdamInOakland I think that saying "no police will ever help us ever" is demonizing. A self-fulfilling prophecy. I haven't given up hope.
@AdamInOakland I think that demonizing police as a whole is an error- excludes potential allies. Where's danger in thanking police support?

RT @oneunderscore__: It's time for journalists to start asking the question: What kind of platform is @jack trying to build?

Talking to Ha…
@AdamInOakland I think that's probably true. Which is why they deserve our support as much as terrible cops deserve our scorn & wrath.
@AdamInOakland With so much pressure for cops to cover each other and do wrong, cops going against the grain are rare and important.
@AdamInOakland My point was that dissenting police can spark change. Exposing riders was important. You don't think more of that is needed?
@AdamInOakland I didn't spend enough time in Oakland to tell, but it seemed to me that Feds looming was only thing bringing faux-change.
@AdamInOakland What about the rookie cop who turned in the riders? I think that he should be applauded. He brought change. @OccupyPolice
@AdamInOakland Again, I don't think we disagree. But I believe that it is possible for that to change. Don't think you do. @OccupyPolice
@OccupyPolice I really respect @AdamInOakland - I think that his views are important and widely-shared. Understanding them is important.
@AdamInOakland I DO agree that the police ARE a massive danger. Which is why I believe thanking dissenters is crucial. @OccupyPolice
@AdamInOakland Maybe where we disagree is in optimism, because I hope to see the day when that happens. No 'absolute evil'. @OccupyPolice
@AdamInOakland I think that that consistency is proof of a system that encourages shitty police, making cops who resist really important.
@AdamInOakland There are lots of cops who hate their COs and their structure more than they hate hippies. I like those. @OccupyPolice
@AdamInOakland In my opinion, any officer who disobeys orders, who puts himself or his job at risk to further goals of Occupy @OccupyPolice
@AdamInOakland The cops in Oakland are unlike any I have ever seen at a protest (from NY to DC) you can tell by how fierce the community is.
@AdamInOakland I come from NY, have dealt with shitty NYPD. Also have friends with NYPD family. Good cops & good people. @OccupyPolice
RT @OccupyPolice: @mrejfox Gotta lift up the good ones & keep the boot on the bad ones, that sends a message about what kind of poli ...
@AdamInOakland I personally spend a lot of time focusing on bad police. I think police who step out of line deserve praise.  @OccupyPolice
@AdamInOakland In Oakland? Probably not. But highlighting good police is the flipside of doxing/publicising bad police, imho @OccupyPolice

As the line between Twitter - the network – and reality itself starts to dissolve, what is the responsibility of Twitter to decide what and what does not happen within it's boundaries? If you want to ban a certain class of speech, it doesn't make it disappear. https://t.co/JbiXWRBtHj

As many people ask for Twitter to make decisions on who should and should not be able to share this online space with us I wonder what we are really asking for, and saying about the way we want to share the internet together https://t.co/0hlcZfQt9O
